title: The Bandwidth Knapsack: Optimal Migration Scheduling for Tiered Memory Systems
description: We formalize page migration scheduling in tiered memory (DRAM/CXL/NVM) as an online knapsack problem, prove that current greedy-by-hotness schedulers have worst-case competitive ratio Omega(R) where R is the page size ratio, and design BKS (Bandwidth Knapsack Scheduler), a theoretically-grounded scheduler achieving O(log R) competitive ratio that composes with any page ranking policy.
motivation: All existing tiered memory systems (TPP, MEMTIS, Colloid, Nomad, SOAR/ALTO) focus on which pages to migrate by ranking pages using hotness, latency, or AOL metrics. However, they treat the complementary question of how to schedule migrations under limited bandwidth as secondary, using simple heuristics (fixed-rate caps, FIFO queuing, ad-hoc throttling). Migration bandwidth is a scarce shared resource -- on CXL hardware, migration traffic competes with regular memory accesses for interconnect bandwidth. When page sizes are heterogeneous (4KB base pages vs 2MB huge pages, a 512x ratio), greedy-by-rank scheduling can waste bandwidth by selecting large low-density pages over many small high-density ones. No existing system formalizes this scheduling problem or provides theoretical performance guarantees.
proposed_approach: We propose BKS (Bandwidth Knapsack Scheduler) with four contributions: (1) Formalize the Bandwidth-Constrained Migration Scheduling (BCMS) problem as an online knapsack variant with renewable capacity; (2) Prove that greedy-by-rank has Omega(R) competitive ratio while our threshold-based benefit-density algorithm achieves O(log R); (3) Augment per-epoch decisions with decay-adjusted benefits using exponentially weighted moving averages to account for temporal access pattern dynamics; (4) Extend to multi-tenant settings via multidimensional knapsack formulation with fairness constraints. BKS is composable -- it takes any page ranking policy's output and optimizes the migration schedule, adding only O(n log n) per-epoch overhead for sorting candidates.

related_work.title: TPP: Transparent Page Placement for CXL-Enabled Tiered-Memory
related_work.authors: Hasan Al Maruf, Hao Wang, Abhishek Dhanotia, Johannes Weiner et al.
related_work.relation: Foundational CXL tiering system using FIFO migration with rate limits. BKS replaces TPP's migration scheduler with a bandwidth-optimal one.

related_work.title: MEMTIS: Efficient Memory Tiering with Dynamic Page Classification and Page Size Determination
related_work.authors: Taehyung Lee, Sumit Kumar Monga, Changwoo Min, Young Ik Eom
related_work.relation: First to consider page size heterogeneity in tiering decisions. BKS addresses the scheduling implication of mixed page sizes that MEMTIS identifies but doesn't optimize for.

related_work.title: Tiered Memory Management: Access Latency is the Key!
related_work.authors: Midhul Vuppalapati, Rachit Agarwal, Dan Ports, Abraham Silberschatz
related_work.relation: Introduces latency-aware page ranking (Colloid) with explicit migration limits. BKS optimizes within those limits using density-based scheduling instead of greedy selection.

related_work.title: Nomad: Non-Exclusive Memory Tiering via Transactional Page Migration
related_work.authors: Weiqian Xiang, Ke Liu, Lei Wang, Ziqi Shuai
related_work.relation: Reduces demotion bandwidth via shadow copies. Complementary: BKS still optimizes promotion bandwidth which Nomad does not address.

related_work.title: Tiered Memory Management Beyond Hotness
related_work.authors: Jinshu Liu, Hamid Hadian, Hanchen Xu, Huaicheng Li
related_work.relation: State-of-the-art page ranking using AOL metric. ALTO regulates migration rates heuristically; BKS provides theoretically optimal scheduling for ALTO's candidates.

related_work.title: Competitive Algorithms for Online Multidimensional Knapsack Problems
related_work.authors: Adam Lechowicz, Bo Sun, Mohammad Hajiesmaili, Adam Wierman
related_work.relation: Provides the theoretical foundation (threshold-based algorithms, competitive ratio proofs) we adapt to the tiered memory migration scheduling setting.

related_work.title: The Online Knapsack Problem with Departures
related_work.authors: Bo Sun, Lin Yang, Mohammad Hajiesmaili, Adam Wierman, John C.S. Lui, Don Towsley, Danny H.K. Tsang
related_work.relation: Extends online knapsack to items with finite lifetimes. Directly models our setting where page hotness changes over time, making candidates 'depart' from the migration queue.

related_work.title: The Design of Competitive Online Algorithms via a Primal-Dual Approach
related_work.venue: Foundations and Trends in Theoretical Computer Science, 2009
related_work.relation: Foundational framework for our competitive analysis proofs.

hypothesis: Greedy-by-rank migration scheduling (the current standard) is provably suboptimal with Omega(R) competitive ratio for heterogeneous page sizes, and a benefit-density threshold scheduler (BKS) achieves O(log R) competitive ratio, translating to 3-12% application performance improvement in bandwidth-constrained tiered memory configurations.
success_criteria: Prove Omega(R) lower bound for greedy-by-rank and O(log R) upper bound for BKS
BKS improves simulated application latency by >= 3% over greedy-by-rank on at least 4 of 6 SPEC workloads under bandwidth-constrained settings
BKS improves performance when composed with at least 2 of 3 page ranking policies (TPP, Colloid, ALTO)
BKS achieves higher Jain's fairness index than equal-share bandwidth partitioning in multi-tenant scenarios
